title: Dungeon Crusade - An Up Hill Battle to Greatness
body:
https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=hVMJN3o6-5g

Daniel (@dungeondive)'s first review. He frames Dungeon Crusade as a **layers-of-simple-systems** game in the lineage of Legends of Andor — anachronistic in places (roll-to-move, randomness that breaks tactics), but genuinely greater than the sum of its parts once you stop fighting it.

What he loves: the **patrol route** system, where colour-coded footprint nodes and a d8 push minions and guardians around the dungeon independently of the heroes — creating little pockets of activity you can plan around or sneak past, instead of the standard "both sides converge and fight" loop. The encounter deck (always something new, environments that persist), the treasure chest deck (genuinely fun to open), the upkeep-phase initiative assignment that splinters six heroes into improvised sub-parties, the difficulty dials, the mining-and-recipe gem loop, and the avalon adventure board game as an interactive book of lore.

What hurts: the rulebook. Verbose, conversational, hard to scan mid-play, and almost certainly under-blind-tested — icon mismatches between monster cards and combat tokens, treasure-chest icons stamped over the critical red raid arrows, dungeons not even labelled by name in the book. He pleads for someone to write a 24-page condensed reference. Until then his recommendation is blunt: read it, play it wrong consistently, *then* go back. Once you crest the rule-book hill the four-to-eight-hour epic dungeon crawl underneath is worth every page you slogged through.

----

Daniel says the game's biggest enemy is its own rulebook. How long are you willing to spend learning a game before the campaign even starts — and what does that ceiling look like for you?
